name: How often does siding really need to be replaced in a foggy, coastal climate?
headline: How often does siding really need to be replaced in a foggy, coastal climate?

mainEntity.name: How often does siding really need to be replaced in a foggy, coastal climate?
mainEntity.text: How often does siding really need to be replaced in a foggy, coastal climate?

acceptedAnswer.text: In coastal and foggy climates, siding replacement is often needed every **25–40 years** depending on the material, maintenance, and how well it was installed. Here are warning signs it’s time to replace, not just patch: - Soft, spongy, or crumbling siding when you press with a screwdriver. - Visible rot, mold, or mildew that comes back repeatedly. - Cracked, warped, or buckled boards in several areas. - Flaking paint and exposed wood across large sections, not just a few spots. - Interior issues like peeling paint, stains, or a musty smell on outside walls. In Pacifica, Daly City, and Half Moon Bay, the constant moisture and salty air speed up wear, especially on older wood and hardboard siding. If you’ve had to repaint more and more often or you keep dealing with leaks around windows, it may be more cost‑effective to do a full replacement. Pitfalls to avoid: - Replacing just a few bad boards while leaving widespread hidden rot behind. - Ignoring flashing and housewrap; the new siding is only as good as what’s underneath. - Assuming siding is fine just because it “looks okay” from far away; close inspection matters. A good rule is to have a knowledgeable person inspect every few years once your siding is past 20 years old.
